comment.body:
Ya that is definitely my definition of a bull trap. Great example because there was MASSIVE conviction and many people were getting caught up in the hysteria. That’s what I think of when stating “bull trap”. 

I think that phrase should really only be applicable on that time frame, otherwise anything smaller you are just in a typical mean reversion/stochastic range.

comment.body: Regarding bull traps,Here is my view of recent giant bull trap.Lots of people got trapped into Trump victory, and Golden Age promises, all dreams come true under Trump. Also notable how market rallied to retes that range and rejected last week. Many laggards that missed Biden's 2y long pump bought the Trump's top/trap,now holding bag. Thats a bull trap, also u can find small bull/bear traps on any time frame, depending on your imagination and interpretation. 👇

comment.body: A comment from the sidelines on potential timing.  As I understand Alma's weekly post (and not trying to put any words in her mouth), she suggests that because in relative terms skew is not bid, the market is underpricing a potential [insert your favorite word -- I'll use sh!tstorm] from the impending tariff issues, both whatever Trump does and however the EU might respond, with an emphasis on under appreciation of how strong the EU's response may be.  In making this observation, she looks at the skew for next week, April 15, and April OpEx.  My inference therefore is that the timeline for such an event -- and the timing of the bull trap setup -- is within 1-3 weeks, not a setup that might take months and months to play out.  This fits in with her broader view of timing that she's referenced repeatedly of a bottoming [note the use of the gerund, not a "bottom" but a "bottoming," i.e., a process] after Easter and into June.  And accepting that the goal of this bottoming is to provide a stable foundation for growth, it seems unlikely to be V-bottom, followed by lockout rally (think Nov 2023).  Putting this together in my mind, a potential downside surprise over the coming weeks precipitating a big drop, which then takes all wind out of the market's sails, the market then churns and grinds lower in a bottoming process through May into June as the geopolitical events Alma has described unfold and potentially resolve.

comment.body:
Guys, I’m glad there’s a discussion going on about what I wrote. The problem is that, first, some people are taking one sentence out of context and trying to interpret everything rigidly. Second, I see some trying to start from the wrong premises, and apparently they haven’t read my writings in their entirety.
A bull trap aims to lure traders and investors expecting prices to rise into a bad trade on both the spot and the time axes.
It’s exactly that situation where the trade seems too good to be true.
By itself, whether a price moves up or down says nothing. The volatility assigned to it, however, does, as does its interaction (correlation) with other assets.
In the current economic structure, there’s a “conventional” or “healthy” dynamic to these. If the dynamic goes off-kilter while the price, for example, shows a bullish trend, then you know something’s off. This might be a spot up / VIX up scenario, or gold, equities, bonds, oil, and JPY all moving in the same direction, etc., while the price might have already blown through three-sigma events… in such cases, you know one of those trends is lying, and it’s up to the analyst to figure out which one.

It’s exactly that situation where the trade seems too good to be true.
By itself, whether a price moves up or down says nothing. The volatility assigned to it, however, does, as does its interaction (correlation) with other assets.
In the current economic structure, there’s a “conventional” or “healthy” dynamic to these. If the dynamic goes off-kilter while the price, for example, shows a bullish trend, then you know something’s off. This might be a spot up / VIX up scenario, or gold, equities, bonds, oil, and JPY all moving in the same direction, etc., while the price might have already blown through three-sigma events… in such cases, you know one of those trends is lying, and it’s up to the analyst to figure out which one.

comment.body: The key is that since algos and rational traders watch these metrics, they end up being fooled by the setup because they’re not interpreting it correctly. Pieces of the puzzle are missing, so they fail to notice that the bull move isn’t a sustainable trend but rather a short-term move that won’t gain momentum and will soon collapse, lacking any supportive investor intent.

comment.body:
In the current situation, my posts have clearly outlined where, in my view, the market is misjudging left-tail risk—it’s too complacent. There’s no need for speculation, since I literally explained it in the weekly post: first, you need to examine the geopolitical re-structuring as well as the world economy’s shift toward friend-shoring.
Relative to that, the market is complacent about tariffs and is hoping for good news on inflation, because it doesn’t understand the underlying intention. In the short term, it doesn’t see that a recession is the plan. Only those who look at SPX/LTY correlation realize this, noticing the lower highs and lower lows trend over the past year—the under‑the‑hood response to stagflation.
Don’t overcomplicate things, please

comment.body: So I'm still little bit confused about intraday RV behavior today especially during AM, below the JPM collar long put strike it is a negative speed profile, with spot up reaching negative gamma the vol is supposed to naturally expand due to dealer hedge flow, but from Alma's GKYZ RV script it seems to be compressed down and seems to me a lower high profile, wouldn't that suggest a hidden selling force which is compressing the RV? Which seems to be the opposite in Alma's last Friday example in which a hidden buying force under positive speed profile when spot down / vol down approaching negative gamma. Hope I would be corrected if I'm thinking wrong.

comment.body: an ITM PUT has negative vanna and you need increasing volatility to break through it. Otherwise MM's will sell to hedge. Buyers come in and are met by this force causing a standstill, due to the size of the position, and compression of RVOL. The position is rolled at 2PM and we take off. RVOL goes up indicating that it was buyers stepping in

Which seems to be the opposite in Alma's last Friday example in which a hidden buying force under positive speed profile when spot down / vol down approaching negative gamma. Hope I would be corrected if I'm thinking wrong.
comment.raw_body: So I'm still little bit confused about intraday RV behavior today especially during AM, below the JPM collar long put strike it is a negative speed profile, with spot up reaching negative gamma the vol is supposed to naturally expand due to dealer hedge flow, but from Alma's GKYZ RV script it seems to be compressed down and seems to me a lower high profile, wouldn't that suggest a hidden selling force which is compressing the RV? Which seems to be the opposite in Alma's last Friday example in which a hidden buying force under positive speed profile when spot down / vol down approaching negative gamma. Hope I would be corrected if I'm thinking wrong.
